@graph.rdfs:comment: The Inference Type describes a discrete observation, assertion, result/conclusion drawn from and/or about a dataset or a collection of data. Note that while the Inference type itself is used to model or capture a primary observation, assertion, result/conclusion akin to a semantic triple; it's schema.org-based structure may also include provenance-related information (akin to provenance-related semantic triples). Lastly, the schema include key Discovery Portal properties to enable improved search.
@graph.rdfs:label: Inference

observationType.description: One or more categories of the inference. The categories may reflect specific triple patterns or motifs. For example, gene x is upregulated in disease y OR gene x is differentiall expressed in disease y.

semanticMapping.description: An explicit mapping of the inference to a SemanticTriple format. Schema.org classes are structured such that each property is a predicate, each Class is the 'subject' and the value of each property is the object. This can be confusing when the class (like the Inference type) can potentially be similar to a semantic triple on its own. While potentially redundant, this property enables explicit and unambiguous mapping of the inference to a semantic triple for increased interpretability.

analyticalMethod.description: One or more analytical methods or techniques used to create the inference from the data

infectiousAgent.description: An infectious agent associated with the inference

species.description: A host organism associated with the inference.

healthCondition.description: A health condition associated with the inference

includedInDataCatalog.description: The source of the inference itself

sample.description: Sample information associated with the inference

observationPeriod.description: The length of time an Observation took place over. The format follows `P[0-9]*[Y|M|D|h|m|s]`. For example, P1Y is Period 1 Year, P3M is Period 3 Months, P3h is Period 3 hours.

duration.description: A time interval in ISO 8601 duration format

temporalType.description: Categorization of how the date/temporal interval relates to the dataset

measurementQualifier.description: Provides additional qualification to an observation. For example, a GDP observation measures the Nominal value.

measuredProperty.description: The measuredProperty of an [[Observation]], typically via its [[StatisticalVariable]]. There are various kinds of applicable [[Property]]: a schema.org property, a property from other RDF-compatible systems, e.g. W3C RDF Data Cube, Data Commons, Wikidata, or schema.org extensions such as [GS1's](https://www.gs1.org/voc/?show=properties).

measurementTechnique.description:
A technique, method or technology used in an [[Observation]], [[StatisticalVariable]] or [[Dataset]] (or [[DataDownload]], [[DataCatalog]]), corresponding to the method used for measuring the corresponding variable(s) (for datasets, described using [[variableMeasured]]; for [[Observation]], a [[StatisticalVariable]]). Often but not necessarily each [[variableMeasured]] will have an explicit representation as (or mapping to) an property such as those defined in Schema.org, or other RDF vocabularies and "knowledge graphs". In that case the subproperty of [[variableMeasured]] called [[measuredProperty]] is applicable.
    
The [[measurementTechnique]] property helps when extra clarification is needed about how a [[measuredProperty]] was measured. This is oriented towards scientific and scholarly dataset publication but may have broader applicability; it is not intended as a full representation of measurement, but can often serve as a high level summary for dataset discovery. 

For example, if [[variableMeasured]] is: molecule concentration, [[measurementTechnique]] could be: "mass spectrometry" or "nmr spectroscopy" or "colorimetry" or "immunofluorescence". If the [[variableMeasured]] is "depression rating", the [[measurementTechnique]] could be "Zung Scale" or "HAM-D" or "Beck Depression Inventory". 

If there are several [[variableMeasured]] properties recorded for some given data object, use a [[PropertyValue]] for each [[variableMeasured]] and attach the corresponding [[measurementTechnique]]. The value can also be from an enumeration, organized as a [[MeasurementMethodEnum]].

measurementDenominator.description: Identifies the denominator variable when an observation represents a ratio or percentage.

measurementMethod.description: A subproperty of [[measurementTechnique]] that can be used for specifying specific methods, in particular via [[MeasurementMethodEnum]].

variableMeasured.description: The variableMeasured property can indicate (repeated as necessary) the  variables that are measured in some dataset, either described as text or as pairs of identifier and description using PropertyValue, or more explicitly as a [[StatisticalVariable]].

observationAbout.description: The [[observationAbout]] property identifies an entity, often a [[Place]], associated with an [[Observation]].

maxValue.description: The upper value of some characteristic or property.

minValue.description: The lower value of some characteristic or property.

unitCode.description: The unit of measurement given using the UN/CEFACT Common Code (3 characters) or a URL. Other codes than the UN/CEFACT Common Code may be used with a prefix followed by a colon.

unitText.description: A string or text indicating the unit of measurement. Useful if you cannot provide a standard unit code for unitCode.

value.description:
The value of a [[QuantitativeValue]] (including [[Observation]]) or property value node.

* For [[QuantitativeValue]] and [[MonetaryAmount]], the recommended type for values is 'Number'.
* For [[PropertyValue]], it can be 'Text', 'Number', 'Boolean', or 'StructuredValue'.
* Use values from 0123456789 (Unicode 'DIGIT ZERO' (U+0030) to 'DIGIT NINE' (U+0039)) rather than superficially similar Unicode symbols.
* Use '.' (Unicode 'FULL STOP' (U+002E)) rather than ',' to indicate a decimal point. Avoid using these symbols as a readability separator.

valueReference.description: A secondary value that provides additional information on the original value, e.g. a reference temperature or a type of measurement.

identifier.description: The identifier property represents any kind of identifier for any kind of [[Thing]], such as ISBNs, GTIN codes, UUIDs etc. Schema.org provides dedicated properties for representing many of these, either as textual strings or as URL (URI) links. See [background notes](/docs/datamodel.html#identifierBg) for more details.

subjectOf.description: A CreativeWork or Event about this Thing.

identifier.description: The identifier property represents any kind of identifier for any kind of Thing, such as ISBNs, GTIN codes, UUIDs etc. Schema.org provides dedicated properties for representing many of these, either as textual strings or as URL (URI) links. See background notes for more details.

termCode.description: A code that identifies this DefinedTerm within a DefinedTermSet.

inDefinedTermSet.description: A DefinedTermSet that contains this term.

originalName.description: The original name of the item, before any metadata standardization/normalization

isCurated.description: A boolean indicator describing whether or not this DefinedTerm was curated as the value of a property.

propertyvalue.description: A property-value pair, e.g. representing a feature of a product or place. Use the 'name' property for the name of the property. If there is an additional human-readable version of the value, put that into the 'description' property.

value.description: The value of a QuantitativeValue (including Observation) or property value node.

unitText.description: A unit associated with the value. E.g.- 'records' for the number of records in a database.

propertyID.description: A commonly used identifier for the characteristic represented by the property...

semantictriple.description: The inference explicitly mapped as a semantic triple

isSymmetric.description: A boolean indicating whether the triple's predicate asserts a symmetric relationship — that is, if A relates to B, then B relates to A by the same predicate. For example, biolink:correlated_with is symmetric, while biolink:regulates is not. Corresponds to owl:SymmetricProperty in OWL and symmetric: true in the Biolink Model.

inversePredicate.description: The predicate that expresses the inverse of this triple's relationship. The predicate which would be used if subject and object were swapped. Only meaningful when isSymmetric is false. For example, the inverse of biolink:regulates is biolink:regulated_by. Corresponds to owl:inverseOf in OWL and the inverse: slot on predicate definitions in the Biolink Model.

tripleObject.description: The value or entity the predicate points to (corresponding to the RDF object).

triplePredicate.description: The relationship being asserted, observed, inferred, between the subject and the object (corresponding to the RDF predicate)

tripleSubject.description: The entity the assertion, observation, or inference is about (corresponding to the RDF subject).

tripleObjectQualifier.description: Contextual or qualifying information about the object of the assertion, observation, or inference. For example, a chemical entity object may be qualified by its form (e.g. abundance or activity of a gene product), or a phenotypic feature object may be qualified by its severity, onset, or frequency in the context of a given disease association. Corresponds to object-level qualifiers in RDF-star annotations or RDF literal metadata.

tripleSubjectQualifier.description: Contextual or qualifying information about the subject of the assertion, observation, or inference. For example, a disease subject may be qualified by the anatomical location or cell type in which it manifests. Corresponds to subject-level qualifiers in RDF-star annotations or named graph metadata.

triplePredicateQualifier.description: Contextual or qualifying information about the predicate of the assertion, observation, or inference. For example, a 'gene associated with disease' predicate may be qualified by the association type (e.g. causal vs. correlated), the direction of effect (e.g. gain-of-function vs. loss-of-function), or the knowledge source (e.g. curated vs. inferred). Corresponds to predicate-level qualifiers in RDF-star annotations or property path metadata.

url.description: Link to data or tool catalog

versionDate.description: date the metadata was harvested from the catalog

archivedAt.description: The url(s) of one or more datasets, tools, resources or other items listed in a DataCatalog or ResourceCatalog

pathogen.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

disease.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

organism.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

includedInDataCatalog.description: Data or resource catalog where the sample is listed

associatedPhenotype.description: one or more observable traits or conditions relevant to the source of the sample.

anyOf.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

items.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

associatedGenotype.description: One or more genetic variants or molecular features observed or inferred from the sample.

anyOf.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

items.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

cellType.description: The type of cell represented in or derived from the biological sample, based on morphology, lineage, or molecular markers.

anyOf.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

items.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

anatomicalSystem.description: Indicate the anatomical system from which the sample is collect, e.g. circulatory, digestive, endocrine, immune and other systems.

anyOf.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

items.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

anatomicalStructure.description: Indicate the organ, tissues the sample was collected from.

anyOf.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

items.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

sex.description: The biological sex of the individual from which the sample was obtained.

anyOf.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

items.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

developmentalStage.description: A temporal subdivision of a developmental process, indicating the age or stage of the object when the sample was created.

anyOf.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

items.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

sampleAvailability.description: A flag to signal that the sample is available for reuse.

sampleQuantity.description: A numeric value for a measurement of the size of a sample. If completely unknown, set value = 0, and unitText = 'unknown'

sampleType.description: The type of sample or specimen.

anyOf.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

items.description: collection of vocabulary terms defined in ontologies

includedInDataCatalog.description: Data or resource catalog where the sample is listed

aggregateElement.description: Aggregate information about the samples in the Sample Collection formatted as a sample object.

itemListElement.description: Samples listed in the sample collection

numberOfItems.description: The number of sample records in the sample collection. Min/max estimates can be used in lieu of actual number. If completely unknown, set value = 0, and unitText = 'unknown'

includedInDataCatalog.description: Data or resource catalog where the sample is listed

quantitativevalue.description: A numeric value and unit for a property with potentially different units

value.description: A numeric value for the property

maxValue.description: An estimated maximum numberic value if the actual value is not known. Ignore if actual numeric value is known.

minValue.description: An estimated minumum numeric value if the actual value is not known. Ignore if actual numeric value is known.

unitText.description: A unit associated with the value. E.g.- 'records' for the number of records in a database.

datePublished.description: Date the referenced work was published

includedInDataCatalog.description: Data or tool catalog which houses the work

identifier.description: An identifier associated with the work

doi.description: A digital object identifier (DOI) of the referenced work

citation.description: The bibliographic citation for the referenced work as is provided

identifier.description: An identifier associated with the work
An identifier associated with the property

statvar.description: A numeric value and unit for a property with potentially different units

identifier.description: An identifier associated with the item

statType.description: Indicates the kind of statistic represented by a StatisticalVariable, e.g. mean, count etc. The value of statType is a property, either from within Schema.org (e.g. median, marginOfError, maxValue, minValue) or from other compatible (e.g. RDF) systems such as DataCommons.org or Wikidata.org.

populationType.description: Indicates the populationType common to all members of a StatisticalPopulation or all cases within the scope of a StatisticalVariable.

constraintProperty.description: Indicates a property used as a constraint. For example, in the definition of a StatisticalVariable. The value is a property, either from within Schema.org or from other compatible (e.g. RDF) systems such as DataCommons.org or Wikidata.org.

@graph.rdfs:comment: The SemanticTriple is a structured representation of a subject–predicate–object assertion, modeled after the RDF triple pattern used in knowledge graphs and linked data systems. While schema.org/Statement provides a general mechanism for reifying claims about entities, SemanticTriple makes the triple structure explicit and unambiguous, enabling direct, lossless translation between schema.org markup and RDF-based knowledge graph formats such as RDF/XML, Turtle, JSON-LD, and N-Triples.
@graph.rdfs:label: SemanticTriple

isSymmetric.description: A boolean indicating whether the triple's predicate asserts a symmetric relationship — that is, if A relates to B, then B relates to A by the same predicate. For example, biolink:correlated_with is symmetric, while biolink:regulates is not. Corresponds to owl:SymmetricProperty in OWL and symmetric: true in the Biolink Model.

inversePredicate.description: The predicate that expresses the inverse of this triple's relationship. The predicate which would be used if subject and object were swapped. Only meaningful when isSymmetric is false. For example, the inverse of biolink:regulates is biolink:regulated_by. Corresponds to owl:inverseOf in OWL and the inverse: slot on predicate definitions in the Biolink Model.

tripleObject.description: The value or entity the predicate points to (corresponding to the RDF object).

triplePredicate.description: The relationship being asserted, observed, inferred, between the subject and the object (corresponding to the RDF predicate)

tripleSubject.description: The entity the assertion, observation, or inference is about (corresponding to the RDF subject).

tripleObjectQualifier.description: Contextual or qualifying information about the object of the assertion, observation, or inference. For example, a chemical entity object may be qualified by its form (e.g. abundance or activity of a gene product), or a phenotypic feature object may be qualified by its severity, onset, or frequency in the context of a given disease association. Corresponds to object-level qualifiers in RDF-star annotations or RDF literal metadata.

tripleSubjectQualifier.description: Contextual or qualifying information about the subject of the assertion, observation, or inference. For example, a disease subject may be qualified by the anatomical location or cell type in which it manifests. Corresponds to subject-level qualifiers in RDF-star annotations or named graph metadata.

triplePredicateQualifier.description: Contextual or qualifying information about the predicate of the assertion, observation, or inference. For example, a 'gene associated with disease' predicate may be qualified by the association type (e.g. causal vs. correlated), the direction of effect (e.g. gain-of-function vs. loss-of-function), or the knowledge source (e.g. curated vs. inferred). Corresponds to predicate-level qualifiers in RDF-star annotations or property path metadata.

identifier.description: The identifier property represents any kind of identifier for any kind of Thing, such as ISBNs, GTIN codes, UUIDs etc. Schema.org provides dedicated properties for representing many of these, either as textual strings or as URL (URI) links. See background notes for more details.

inDefinedTermSet.description: A DefinedTermSet that contains this term.

originalName.description: The original name of the item, before any metadata standardization/normalization

isCurated.description: A boolean indicator describing whether or not this DefinedTerm was curated as the value of a property.

propertyvalue.description: A property-value pair, e.g. representing a feature of a product or place. Use the 'name' property for the name of the property. If there is an additional human-readable version of the value, put that into the 'description' property.

value.description: The value of a QuantitativeValue (including Observation) or property value node.

unitText.description: A unit associated with the value. E.g.- 'records' for the number of records in a database.

propertyID.description: A commonly used identifier for the characteristic represented by the property...

This can be confusing when the class (like the Inference type) can potentially be similar to a semantic triple on its own.
